editorial_angle: The free market for AI infrastructure is over. This week the US government didn't just draw a line between cleared and uncleared AI vendors — it enforced that line with a sole-source mandate on one side and felony charges on the other. The deeper shift isn't about Palantir winning or Super Micro losing. It's that the criteria for picking AI tools has permanently acquired a column that no analyst report warned about: government alignment status. Solo builders and enterprise buyers alike are now choosing teams in a geopolitical contest they never agreed to enter. The scarce resource was always human judgment. Now it has a security clearance attached.
key_message: The criteria for choosing AI tools just changed from 'what ships best' to 'which side of the line are you on' — and that line is now enforced with criminal prosecution.

visual_metaphor_direction.core_tension: A border crossing checkpoint where AI tools and hardware are being sorted into two fates: cleared vendors sail through with a stamped military passport, while smuggled GPU hardware is seized at the gate by federal agents. Two simultaneous transactions — one a gold-sealed contract, one a confiscation — happening at the same border crossing at the same moment.
key_elements: Palantir logo/branding on one side of the checkpoint — prominently stamped CLEARED or receiving a golden Pentagon seal
Crates or server racks labeled Super Micro being seized, red SEIZED stickers, federal agents present
A hard border dividing line — customs checkpoint, gate, or wall — visually splitting the two fates
GPU chips or AI hardware as the physical object being sorted, not software or models
Pentagon eagle or military insignia on the cleared side — authority, not threat
avoid: Generic robots or AI avatars — this is about hardware and procurement, not sci-fi AI
China or Chinese imagery — the story is about the enforcement mechanism, not geopolitics as a headline
Individual faces or caricatures of Charles Liang or any named person — this is systemic, not personal
Dollar signs or money as the primary symbol — the line is about clearance, not cost
Friendly or optimistic lighting — this should feel tense and high-stakes, not triumphant

x.hook: AI tool selection just got a clearance requirement.
x.angle: The government didn't warn the market — it mandated and prosecuted in the same week. Every AI vendor you evaluate now has a geopolitical status whether the vendor advertises it or not. That's the hot take that drives quote-tweets: the free market for AI infrastructure quietly ended Thursday.

linkedin.hook: How many vendor evaluation frameworks include 'government security clearance status'? Mine didn't. It does now.
linkedin.angle: For CTOs, VPs of Engineering, and procurement leads: the Pentagon mandate and the Super Micro indictment landed in the same week because they're the same policy move — one carrot, one stick. The question for enterprise AI buyers is which column in your evaluation matrix accounts for 'government alignment risk.' Drive comments by asking what criteria readers actually use.

bluesky.hook: I pick tools based on what ships the best code. That just got a lot more complicated.
bluesky.angle: Talking to fellow builders — the personal angle. I use Claude Code daily, Anthropic is in a DOJ lawsuit, Palantir just got the military mandate. I didn't sign up to carry geopolitical baggage in my toolbox, but here we are. Invite peers to share what criteria they actually use, or whether this even registers for solo devs yet.

do_not_include: White House AI legislative framework — policy story but lower engagement than the Pentagon/Super Micro thread
NVIDIA Agent Toolkit at GTC — enterprise infrastructure, not the same narrative
Alibaba Wukong launch — adjacent geopolitics but different story
Anthropic DOJ lawsuit specifics — mention the clearance dimension only, don't make it the Anthropic story
Speculation on whether Palantir's mandate survives administration changes — open question, not a stated position
